description: `cancel_teardown()` cancels the group's teardown; it does nothing to the nodes (#lzspecedgeindex). The operation changes only what the group does at end-of-life — node state, edges, and values are untouched, which is why it is named for the teardown rather than for the nodes. After calling it, the group's nodes remain fully readable, keep their edges, keep propagating, and remain individually disposable; ending the group afterwards disposes nothing at all. What breaks if a binding gets this wrong: implemented as a dispose-now it destroys the very nodes the caller asked to keep; implemented as a no-op on the *group* rather than on its owned set, the group still tears down at end-of-life and the escaped handles dangle. The distinguishing observation is the pair of steps after `end_group` — the nodes must still be readable *and* must still respond to a publish, which rules out a binding that merely suppresses the error and leaves the nodes half-detached. The final steps pin that cancelling teardown does not make the nodes undisposable: they revert to plain context ownership, the same state every ungrouped node is already in, and individual disposal still works normally.

expect.note: cancelling teardown disposes nothing and detaches nothing — the edge sets are exactly what they were one step ago. The group now owns no nodes, so its end-of-life is a no-op.
ending a group whose teardown was cancelled disposes nothing
the nodes still propagate — a binding that half-detached them would show a stale value or a silent effect here
nodes whose group teardown was cancelled revert to plain context ownership and stay individually disposable
